metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command or test reference for each AC (e.g., `pytest tests/lander/test_s3_failure.py -k recheck_bounded` for AC1; a Gerrit mock-API sequence for AC4 409 path; a restart-replay assertion for AC7) so every criterion has an observable, in-session verification step.
findings.finding: No AC maps to a concrete proving command or verification step. The plan specifies seven ACs describing bot behaviors (recheck state machine, Autosubmit removal, marker persistence, idempotent restart) but provides zero verification commands — no test invocation, no Gerrit API call sequence, no log-assertion pattern — leaving an executor unable to demonstrate each criterion is met.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all seven bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: For each AC, append a 'Verified by:' line naming the test class/method or curl sequence. Example for AC1: 'Verified by: `pytest tests/lander/test_recheck_state_machine.py::test_single_retry_bound` passes; confirm exactly one `recheck` comment posted to mock Gerrit and no second post after transient Verified=0.' Example for AC4: 'Verified by: inject HTTP 409 from mock `/rebase` endpoint; assert `needs_rebase` marker written with correct SHA before Autosubmit label-removal calls.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a `recheck_posted: true` field to the persisted `needs_rebase`-style marker (or a separate lightweight flag file keyed by `change_id + patchset_SHA`) so the one-recheck-per-patchset bound survives a bot restart, and update AC7 to cover restart-during-recheck idempotency.
findings.finding: The `rechecking` in-flight flag is described as living on the in-memory `wipChain` (not in the persisted store), so a bot restart during the recheck window would lose this flag; on restart the bot re-reads Gerrit, sees Verified=0 (the clear-vote transient), has no flag indicating a recheck is already in-flight, and may re-post `recheck` — violating the 'bounded to one' constraint the AC explicitly requires.
findings.location: AC1 — Recheck trigger + state machine (concrete); Outcome record section
findings.suggested_fix: Add `recheck_posted_at: <ISO timestamp>` to the persisted marker shape (written atomically when the `recheck` comment is posted, before awaiting the vote). On restart, if the marker exists and `recheck_posted_at` is set and the patchset SHA still matches, the bot skips re-posting and enters the waiting-for-terminal-vote state directly. Document this in AC1 and AC7.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the upsert key in the Outcome record section and the Idempotent ordering AC: decide whether the store is keyed by `change_id + patchset_SHA` (one record per change) or by `stack_id` (one record per stack), and state the same key in both sections.
findings.finding: The upsert/overwrite key for the needs_rebase marker is stated differently in two sections: the Outcome record section specifies `change_id + patchset_SHA` as the key ('A re-run overwrites the prior marker for the same `change_id + patchset_SHA`'), while the Idempotent ordering AC specifies `stack_id` as the upsert key ('the `stack_id` upsert key makes re-runs overwrite rather than duplicate'). These are different granularities — a stack can contain multiple changes, each with its own `change_id + patchset_SHA`. An implementer reading both sections would not know which key governs overwrite behavior.
findings.location: Outcome record section ('A re-run overwrites the prior marker for the same `change_id + patchset_SHA`') vs. AC Idempotent ordering ('the `stack_id` upsert key makes re-runs overwrite rather than duplicate')
findings.suggested_fix: Since a stack can have multiple changes and the rebase-conflict AC says to record 'a needs_rebase outcome event-driven on that change/stack,' the most consistent interpretation is one record per change keyed by `change_id + patchset_SHA`. Update the Idempotent ordering AC to read: 'the `change_id + patchset_SHA` upsert key makes re-runs overwrite rather than duplicate' (dropping the `stack_id` key claim), and clarify that on restart the bot scans all unacknowledged markers regardless of stack.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike or integration fixture (e.g., a test harness that simulates the Verified -1 → 0 → +1/-1 vote sequence with deliberate timing gaps) to validate the rechecking sub-state machine before full build-out, specifically covering the transient-0 detection and restart-recovery cases.
findings.finding: The recheck state machine's correctness — specifically the bot treating a transient Verified=0 (from clear-vote at CI run start) as 'recheck-running' rather than 'no result' — is asserted without any validation plan. This is the project's own integration code relying on a timing assumption about gerrit-verify.yaml's job ordering and the bot's polling cycle. A race where the bot polls between 'recheck comment posted' and 'clear-vote job fires' could cause the bot to incorrectly interpret the pre-recheck -1 as still live, or vice versa. No spike, RED fixture, or integration probe is planned.
findings.location: AC: 'CI-flake auto-recheck' — 'rechecking in-flight sub-state' and 'MUST treat this transient 0 as recheck-running'
findings.suggested_fix: Write a RED integration test that replays the Gerrit vote event sequence (−1 at T0, recheck comment at T1, 0 at T2, terminal vote at T3) against a mock Gerrit API and asserts the bot's state transitions at each poll interval. Cover the restart case by checkpointing and replaying from T2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the auto-recheck retry limit configurable (e.g., a project-infra config key such as `auto_lander.recheck_max_attempts`, defaulting to 1) rather than hardcoding `1` in the loop logic.
findings.finding: The '1 retry' recheck bound is hardcoded in the spec with no provision for configurability. CI flakiness profiles vary by project and over time; teams may want 0 retries (hand back immediately) or 2+ retries for high-flake environments. A hardcoded value will require a code change to tune.
findings.location: AC: 'CI-flake auto-recheck' — 'bounded auto-recheck — 1 retry on the SAME patchset'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `recheck_max_attempts: 1` to the auto-lander config schema; the loop reads this value and the in-flight flag tracks attempt count against it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or a CLAUDE.md / design-doc section) capturing the 'Gerrit-as-source-of-truth / marker-as-cache' architectural decision, the self-invalidating SHA semantics, and the acknowledged-flag restart protocol, so future maintainers and S4/S9 consumers understand the contract.
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural decisions — a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache with self-invalidating patchset-SHA markers, a rechecking in-flight sub-state on wipChain, and the stack-atomic hand-back protocol — but does not include a corresponding ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry. Future maintainers encountering the marker shape or the acknowledged flag without context will need to reverse-engineer these decisions from code.
findings.location: 'Outcome record' section; AC: 'Persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache'; AC: 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a maximum recheck wait timeout (e.g., `recheck_timeout_minutes`, suggested default matching the typical CI run duration) after which the bot treats the recheck as failed, records `ci_failed`, and strips Autosubmit — preventing indefinite stalls.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that after posting a `recheck` comment, the bot 'waits for the terminal vote' but states no timeout for this wait. If CI stalls mid-run (e.g., the clear-vote job fires, resetting to 0, but the verify job never completes), the bot's rechecking sub-state has no exit condition other than the terminal vote — the stack is blocked indefinitely and the loop cannot progress.
findings.location: AC: 'CI-flake auto-recheck' — 'waits for the terminal vote — +1 → clear the flag; -1 → record ci_failed'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `recheck_deadline` field to the rechecking sub-state (set to `now + recheck_timeout`); on each loop tick, if `now > recheck_deadline` and still in rechecking state, treat as `-1` and proceed to hand-back.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a maximum wait timeout for the `rechecking` sub-state (e.g., 2× typical CI wall-clock time) and define the escape action (treat as `ci_failed`, hand back) with a log/alert when the timeout fires; also specify whether the `rechecking` flag is durably persisted across restarts.
findings.finding: The `rechecking` sub-state has no timeout or escape hatch: if CI never posts a terminal vote after the `recheck` comment (e.g., the verify workflow hangs or crashes before voting), the bot stays in `rechecking` forever with no specified way to detect, surface, or recover from this condition.
findings.location: AC — 'CI-flake auto-recheck' bullet: 'it then enters a rechecking in-flight sub-state … and waits for the terminal vote'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'If no terminal vote arrives within T minutes (T configurable, default 2× expected CI duration), the bot exits `rechecking`, records `ci_failed`, removes Autosubmit from the stack, and emits an alert-level log event. The `rechecking` flag is persisted alongside the needs_rebase marker (or as a field on it) so a restart can restore the sub-state and honour the one-retry bound.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry/backoff behavior (e.g., exponential backoff with a max-attempt cap) for all Gerrit REST calls in the failure paths (POST /rebase, label removal, comment posting), and define what happens when retries are exhausted (log + alert + leave state durable for next restart pass).
findings.finding: Error handling for Gerrit REST API calls (POST /rebase, label removal beyond 404/409, comment posting for `recheck`) is unspecified for network errors and 5xx responses: the plan only documents the 404/409 no-op, leaving all other error codes and transient failures without a defined behavior.
findings.location: AC — 'Rebase-conflict' bullet and 'CI-fail' bullet; 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart' AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at minimum one structured metric or counter per failure-path outcome (ci_failed, needs_rebase, recheck_triggered, recheck_succeeded, autosubmit_removal_failed) and specify an alert condition for anomalous rates or stuck recheck sub-states.
findings.finding: Observability for the new failure paths is limited to 'a rolling per-transition log line for debugging'; no metrics, structured events, or alerts are specified for failure rates (CI-fail rate, rebase-conflict rate), stuck-recheck counts, or Autosubmit-removal failures — making it impossible for operators to know if the failure-handling machinery itself is broken.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'A rolling per-transition log line is kept for debugging'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE/cleanup policy for acknowledged markers (e.g., purge after N days, or purge when the associated change_id is no longer open in Gerrit), and specify where this cleanup runs (startup sweep, periodic job, or inline on read).
findings.finding: There is no specified RETIRE/cleanup path for acknowledged `needs_rebase` marker files: once `acknowledged=true`, the marker remains on disk indefinitely; over time (many stacks, many failures) these accumulate without bound.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'acknowledged': writer sets acknowledged=false … and true after Autosubmit-removal'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the atomic-write mechanism for marker files (e.g., write-to-temp-then-rename, or an exclusive advisory lock) to guarantee that concurrent writers cannot produce a partially-written or interleaved marker.
findings.finding: The atomicity of writing the `needs_rebase` marker file is not specified: if two bot instances (or two concurrent restart passes) process the same 409 simultaneously, the upsert-overwrite semantics rely on the underlying file-write being atomic, which is not guaranteed for typical file I/O without an explicit mechanism (rename-into-place, advisory lock, or a transactional store).
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'A re-run overwrites the prior marker for the same change_id + patchset_SHA'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a write-to-temp-file-then-atomic-rename pattern (standard POSIX guarantee) and document it in the plan so implementers know this is required, not optional.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing section (or per-AC test notes) covering at minimum: (1) recheck state machine — transient Verified→0 does NOT trigger a second recheck; (2) bounded retry — exactly one recheck per patchset, not zero and not two; (3) CI-fail path after recheck — Autosubmit stripped from all stack changes; (4) 409 path — needs_rebase marker written, Autosubmit stripped; (5) self-invalidating marker — mismatched SHA causes marker to be ignored; (6) idempotent restart — unacknowledged marker re-drives removal to completion; (7) 404/409 on removal treated as no-op.
findings.finding: The plan introduces substantial new testable logic — the CI-flake auto-recheck state machine (including the transient `Verified→0` 'recheck-running' guard), the `ci_failed`/`needs_rebase` outcome paths, Autosubmit-removal idempotency under retries/restart, and self-invalidating patchset-SHA markers — but specifies zero tests anywhere in the document. No unit, integration, or end-to-end tests are named, referenced, or described for any of these behaviors.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all ACs); plan as a whole
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Testing' section to the plan with named test scenarios for each AC. For the in-process state-machine logic (transient-0 guard, bounded-retry counter, marker invalidation), offline unit tests with a stubbed Gerrit HTTP client are sufficient and appropriate — the proxy-validation exemption applies because there is no live/external boundary being bypassed. Concretely: write a parameterized test table over vote sequences (e.g., `[-1] → recheck → [0, +1]`, `[-1] → recheck → [0, -1]`, `[-1, 0] with rechecking=true`) asserting the correct next action; add a restart test that writes an `acknowledged=false` marker and verifies the removal is re-driven on startup.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'S4 already does this' with a verifiable contract: either cite the S4 ticket ID and the specific AC that owns live Gerrit derivation of `ci_failed`/`review_failed`, or add an explicit ordering constraint that S3 is not considered complete until S4's live-read implementation exists.
findings.finding: The plan uses 'S4 already does this' as a scope-exclusion justification for NOT persisting `ci_failed`/`review_failed` — asserting those outcomes are derived live from Gerrit at read time by S4. However, S4 is a future, unimplemented story: `infra/autolander/loop.py` does not exist (confirmed by S2's plan-review signature 'dep absent infra/autolander/loop.py'), and no autolander code exists in the repo. This is an unverifiable forward-reference used as a codebase exclusion: if S4's live-read logic is not yet built when S3 ships, the scope exclusion silently deletes the persistence work with no fallback, leaving consumers (S4, S9) without any outcome record for CI-fail and review-fail cases.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'merged / ci_failed / review_failed are NOT persisted — they are derived LIVE from Gerrit at read time (S4 already does this).'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Outcome record section, replace '(S4 already does this)' with '(S4 ticket `<id>` AC `<n>` owns this live-read; S3 depends on that AC being delivered before S3 is considered complete)' — and add S4 as an explicit downstream dependency with the AC reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) by ticket ID and file path in the plan, and explicitly list them as edit targets that must be updated to handle the new marker fields (`acknowledged`, `stack_id`, `change_ids[]`, `failing_change_id?`, `tested_sha`).
findings.finding: S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) are named as live consumers of the S3-produced marker schema and state-dir, but neither their file paths nor ticket IDs are enumerated anywhere in the plan. S4 reads `needs_rebase` markers to determine stack state at `land` time; S9 reads the same cache. Because the plan introduces a new marker shape (`acknowledged`, `stack_id`, `change_ids[]`, `failing_change_id?`, `tested_sha`) any S4/S9 reader code must be updated in sync, but those files are not listed as edit targets.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache + live Gerrit state as the outcome.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Persist the `rechecking` attempt count (or a `recheck_posted_at` timestamp) in the `needs_rebase` marker or a lightweight co-located file so the one-recheck-per-patchset bound survives restarts; on recovery, a flag present + Verified=-1 → treat as post-recheck failure and hand back directly rather than issuing a second recheck.
findings.finding: The `rechecking` in-memory sub-state flag does not survive a process restart, breaking the bounded-one-recheck guarantee. The plan specifies the flag lives on `wipChain` (in-memory, 'NOT a new S2 phase'), yet if the bot crashes or restarts after posting the `recheck` comment but before the terminal vote arrives, the flag is lost. On restart, the bot re-reads Gerrit state: if it sees `Verified=0` (clear-vote ran, CI still running) it has no signal that it already issued a recheck and may either stall indefinitely or re-post `recheck`, exceeding the one-retry bound; if it sees `Verified=-1` (CI completed during downtime) it again cannot distinguish first-failure from post-recheck-failure and would issue a second recheck. The plan otherwise specifies careful restart recovery (acknowledged=false re-drives removal), making this asymmetry a mechanism gap.
findings.location: AC1 — 'Recheck trigger + state machine (concrete)' / rechecking sub-state; Outcome record section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `recheck_count: int` (or `recheck_posted_patchset_sha: str | None`) field to the persisted marker (written to the state dir atomically when the bot posts the `recheck` comment, before entering the `rechecking` sub-state). On restart, if this field is set and equals the current patchset SHA, the bot knows a recheck was already issued: Verified=-1 → hand back immediately; Verified=0 → wait for terminal vote without re-posting.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify against `lfit/releng-gerrit_to_platform@44d5d46` `comment-added.py` (or equivalent) that the g2p `comment-added` hook dispatches `gerrit-verify.yaml` when the commenter is the bot's own Gerrit service account — i.e., confirm there is no author-origin filter that silently drops bot-posted comments.
findings.finding: The plan's auto-recheck mechanism relies on an unverified capability: that g2p's `comment-added` hook will dispatch `gerrit-verify.yaml` when a **bot/service-account** posts the `recheck` comment. The `[mapping "comment-added"] recheck = verify` mapping in the ini template is confirmed, but g2p's `comment-added` script (lfit/releng-gerrit_to_platform@44d5d46, external) may filter out comments from service accounts or the change's own uploader to prevent feedback loops — this is a common Gerrit CI safety pattern. The plan cites g2p's patchset_created.py / github.py behavior but never checks comment-added.py's author-filtering logic for this bot-as-commenter scenario.
findings.location: AC1 — CI-flake auto-recheck: 'the bot triggers CI re-execution by posting the exact Gerrit comment `recheck`'
findings.suggested_fix: Inspect `comment-added.py` in `lfit/releng-gerrit_to_platform@44d5d46` for any `author`, `author-email`, or account-type filter. If a filter exists that would block the bot's comment, the recheck trigger must use a different mechanism (e.g., the Gerrit REST API's `POST /changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/review` endpoint to force a re-run marker, or a direct GitHub Actions `workflow_dispatch` API call from the bot). Document the verified behavior in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (against the g2p `comment_added.py` source at `lfit/releng-gerrit_to_platform@44d5d46`) that the `comment-added` hook dispatches `gerrit-verify.yaml` when the commenter is a service-account/bot Gerrit user, and document the finding in the plan or a companion evidence file; if g2p does filter bot authors, name the workaround (e.g., use a different trigger mechanism or ensure the autolander bot account is in any allowlist).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the bot posts comments as its own Gerrit user; no extra grant needed' and that g2p's `comment-added` hook will dispatch `gerrit-verify.yaml` when the autolander bot posts `recheck`. However, whether g2p's `comment_added.py` script (from `lfit/releng-gerrit_to_platform@44d5d46`) dispatches when the commenter is a service-account/bot user — as opposed to a human contributor — is unverified in this repo. All prior verified `recheck` uses in the repository evidence (CONTRIBUTING.md, runbooks, live change evidence in docs/88ab-feature-branch-evidence.md) are human-posted. If g2p filters out bot-authored `comment-added` events (a common anti-loop design in CI systems), the AC1 auto-recheck mechanism silently fails: the `recheck` comment is posted but no `gerrit-verify.yaml` re-dispatch occurs, leaving the stack permanently in the `rechecking` sub-state.
findings.location: AC1 — CI-flake auto-recheck: 'the bot posts comments as its own Gerrit user; no extra grant needed'
findings.suggested_fix: Read `comment_added.py` at commit `44d5d46` in `lfit/releng-gerrit_to_platform` and check whether it gates dispatch on commenter identity. If it does not filter, add a one-line note to the plan citing the source + line. If it does filter, the plan must specify an alternative trigger (e.g., triggering CI via the Gerrit SSH `review` command or a dedicated REST endpoint rather than a `recheck` comment).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or annotate the marker schema) specifying exactly when `failing_change_id?` is set — e.g., 'set to the change whose Verified=-1 triggered ci_failed; null for needs_rebase' — and confirm S4/S9 consume it correctly.
findings.finding: The marker schema field `failing_change_id?` is defined in the Outcome record section as part of the marker shape, but no acceptance criterion or decision rule anywhere in the plan specifies when this field is populated (ci_failed path? rebase-conflict path? both?), what validates its value, or which downstream consumer (S4 or S9) reads it and how. An executor implementing the marker writer has no specification to follow for this field.
findings.location: Outcome record section — marker shape: `{change_id, patchset_SHA, stack_id, change_ids[], failing_change_id?, tested_sha, recorded_at, acknowledged}`
findings.suggested_fix: In the Outcome record section, annotate: `failing_change_id?` — set to the `change_id` of the change that received `Verified -1` on ci_failed path; omitted/null on the needs_rebase path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a timeout to the `rechecking` sub-state (e.g., 2× the expected CI runtime), after which the bot treats the absence of a terminal vote as a ci_failed condition and proceeds to hand back — this is the required fallback for an unresolvable sub-step per T8(d).
findings.finding: The `rechecking` in-flight sub-state flag placed on `wipChain` has no timeout or escape hatch. If the recheck-triggered gerrit-verify.yaml run is cancelled (GitHub's `cancel-in-progress: true` on the `gerrit-verify-${GERRIT_CHANGE_ID}` concurrency group fires if any other event for the same change triggers a new run), the workflow dies without casting any vote. Verified stays at 0. The bot is stuck in `rechecking` state indefinitely — no terminal vote ever arrives, and AC1 explicitly prohibits re-posting `recheck` when Verified=0.
findings.location: AC1 — 'Recheck trigger + state machine (concrete)' / .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml lines 40-43

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the `acknowledged` field lifecycle (set false at record time; set true only after all Autosubmit removals in the stack complete; restart re-drives if false) into a dedicated AC bullet or explicitly cross-reference it from AC3/AC4/AC7 so the implementation contract is self-contained in the ACs.
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field's population and transition protocol is specified exclusively in the Outcome record prose section, completely divorced from the acceptance criteria that govern marker persistence (AC3, AC4, AC7). Neither AC3 ('persist ONLY needs_rebase markers') nor AC4 ('record a needs_rebase outcome') nor AC7 ('idempotent ordering') mentions the `acknowledged` field or its two-value lifecycle (false→true). An executor reading only the ACs — the normal implementation contract — has no specification for `acknowledged`.
findings.location: Outcome record section ('acknowledged': the writer sets acknowledged=false ... acknowledged=true after Autosubmit-removal') vs AC3, AC4, AC7

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `rechecking` flag on wipChain concretely: specify the field name (e.g., `recheck_posted: bool`), when it is set (after posting the `recheck` comment), when it is cleared (on receipt of terminal +1 or on hand-back after -1), and how restart handles it (treat outstanding flag as 'recheck in-flight, await terminal vote').
findings.finding: The `rechecking` sub-state flag placed on `wipChain` is referenced as a concrete implementation artifact ('an S3-owned flag on the wipChain') but its schema is never defined: no field name, no type (boolean? timestamp?), no initial value, and no specification of which poll iteration clears it on success (+1). The vote-sequence decision rule ('treat Verified=0 as recheck-running') is inlined in AC1 prose but is entirely dependent on this undefined flag being set — making the rule structurally incomplete.
findings.location: AC1 — 'Recheck trigger + state machine (concrete)': 'an S3-owned flag on the wipChain, NOT a new S2 phase'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1 or the Outcome record: 'wipChain gains a boolean field `recheck_posted` (default false). Set to true immediately after posting the recheck comment. Clear to false on receipt of Verified +1 (proceed to submit) or -1 (proceed to hand-back). On restart with recheck_posted=true: resume awaiting terminal vote (do not re-post recheck).'

findings.finding: The auto-recheck mechanism has an unspecified trigger path and a hidden state-machine gap: (a) the plan never specifies HOW the bot triggers CI re-execution — in this codebase 'recheck' is triggered by posting a Gerrit comment that fires the g2p comment-added hook → workflow_dispatch (confirmed in gerrit_to_platform.ini.template `recheck = verify` and gerrit-verify.yaml's concurrency group); the bot needs comment-posting permission and must post the exact comment text; (b) after the recheck comment is posted, gerrit-verify.yaml's clear-vote job resets Verified→0 BEFORE the new vote arrives, so the bot's loop will observe Verified=0 mid-run — the plan says 'still shows Verified -1' as the trigger for hand-back, but after a recheck the vote transiently becomes 0, not -1; the bot must not treat the transient 0 as 'recheck complete with no result' nor re-trigger another recheck loop iteration; the plan specifies no intermediate 'recheck in-flight' state for the S2/S3 state machine.
findings.location: AC: CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back); Outcome record section
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC and Outcome record, add: 'The bot triggers recheck by POSTing a comment containing exactly `recheck` on the failing change via the Gerrit REST API (`POST /changes/{id}/revisions/current/review` with `{"message": "recheck"}`). After posting, the loop enters a `recheck_in_flight` sub-state and waits until `Verified` returns to a non-zero value (the clear-vote job transiently resets it to 0; a stable +1 or -1 marks completion of the recheck). Only then is the retry-count incremented and the result evaluated.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
